perturbation.original_text: FCC will defend, indemnify and hold harmless Flotek, its Affiliates, and their respective officers and employees from all claims, actions, losses, damages and expenses resulting from any injury to persons, damage to property or action by any regulatory agency, arising out of or in any way associated with the operation of the Facility or any other facility of FCC, including, without limitation, injuries to FCC's employees involved in these operations REGARDLESS OF THE NEGLIGENCE OF ANY OF THE INDEMNIFIED PARTIES.
perturbation.changed_text: FCC will endeavor to defend, indemnify and hold harmless Flotek, its Affiliates, and their respective officers and employees from claims, actions, losses, damages and expenses resulting from potential injury to persons, possible damage to property or theoretical action by any regulatory agency, tangentially associated with the operation of the Facility or any other facility of FCC. It is understood that such indemnification is not required in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct by the Flotek.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces 'will defend, indemnify, and hold harmless' with 'will endeavor to defend, indemnify, and hold harmless,' weakening the obligation. The change from 'all claims' to 'claims ... resulting from potential injury' limits the scope of indemnification. The added exclusion for gross negligence/willful misconduct creates ambiguity about when indemnification applies, particularly conflicting with the original 'REGARDLESS OF THE NEGLIGENCE OF ANY OF THE INDEMNIFIED PARTIES.'
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law, which governs this contract according to section 27, generally enforces broad indemnification agreements unless they are unconscionable. The added exceptions and weakening of the language undermine the clarity required for such agreements.
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware General Corporation Law, Title 8, Section 145
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law allows for broad indemnification, including for negligence, but the modified clause introduces uncertainty about the scope of FCC's responsibility, and creates an exception for negligence which is contrary to the original intent. This ambiguity makes it difficult to enforce the indemnification, conflicting with the purpose of Section 145 of the DGCL.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a director may provide that it is i
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding FCC's indemnification obligations, potentially conflicting with the original intent of broad indemnification, even for negligence. The original text stated indemnification 'REGARDLESS OF THE NEGLIGENCE OF ANY OF THE INDEMNIFIED PARTIES'. The changed text weakens this by using 'endeavor to defend' and introducing an exclusion for gross negligence/willful misconduct, creating uncertainty and potentially contradicting the scope of indemnification intended by Delaware law as supported by snippet 1, which discusses the powers and duties of a board of directors, suggesting a comprehensive framework that could be undermined by ambiguous indemnification clauses.

perturbation.original_text: Each party hereto agrees to comply with all applicable federal, state provincial and or local environmental law, ordinances, codes, rules, regulations and permits and to handle all raw materials, off specification product, excess or scrap materials, waste, and finished products in an environmentally safe manner so as to prevent any contamination of the structure, soil or ground water in, on, or adjacent to its premises.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall attempt to comply with relevant environmental guidelines and shall endeavor to handle materials in a reasonably safe manner, taking into account economic feasibility, to mitigate potential environmental impacts within the constraints of available resources.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates compliance with 'all applicable' environmental laws. The modified text weakens this to 'attempt to comply with relevant environmental guidelines' and introduces subjective factors like 'economic feasibility' and 'available resources,' creating ambiguity. This contradicts the strict compliance required by many environmental regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCRA mandates strict 'cradle-to-grave' management of hazardous waste. The modified text allows for economic considerations to override environmental safety, potentially leading to improper handling of hazardous waste and violations of RCRA's regulatory requirements.

Permits for treatment, storage, or disposal of hazardous waste (a) Permit requirements Not later than eighteen months after October 21, 1976, the Administrator shall promulgate regulations requiring each person owning or operating an existing facility or planning to construct a new facility for the treatment, storage, or dispo
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates strict compliance with all environmental laws, whereas the modified text introduces ambiguity and subjective considerations such as 'economic feasibility,' potentially leading to non-compliance with environmental regulations. The Law Explanation references RCRA, highlighting the 'cradle-to-grave' management of hazardous waste, which is undermined by allowing economic factors to compromise environmental safety. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 42 USC 6925, pertains to permits for treatment, storage, or disposal of hazardous waste. While the snippet primarily discusses permit requirements, it establishes a framework for the regulated handling of hazardous waste. The change from 'comply with all applicable' to 'attempt to comply' weakens the obligation, and introducing economic feasibility as a consideration directly undermines the strict regulatory intent implied in the RCRA framework and Snippet 1 by potentially allowing cost to justify non-compliance. This creates a legal contradiction as it introduces an ambiguous legal obligation, making it difficult to enforce the original intent under state or national law.

perturbation.original_text: FCC warrants to Flotek that: (a) all of the Terpene Product supplied by FCC to Flotek shall: (i) conform to the specifications set forth in Section 1; 3 (ii) comply with all relevant laws and regulations including, without limitation, laws and regulations of each of the jurisdictions in which the Terpene Product are either manufactured or to be sold or used concerning purity, sanitation, safety, security, and packaging and labeling of food and beverage; (iii) be in good condition at the time of delivery in all respects; and (iv) be free from any defect in design, workmanship, materials and packaging; and (b) it shall convey to Flotek good title to the Terpene Product free of any encumbrance, lien or security interest;
perturbation.changed_text: FCC believes to Flotek that: (a) the Terpene Product supplied by FCC to Flotek will strive to: (i) generally conform to the specifications set forth in Section 1; 3 (ii) aim to comply with pertinent laws and regulations including laws of the manufacturing jurisdiction concerning basic sanitation, general safety, and standard packaging of food and beverage; (iii) usually be in acceptable condition at the time of delivery; and (iv) be reasonably free from major defects in workmanship and packaging; and (b) it attempts to convey to Flotek reasonably good title to the Terpene Product.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'warrants' with 'believes,' 'shall' with 'will strive to,' 'comply' with 'aim to comply,' 'good condition' with 'acceptable condition,' and 'free from any defect' with 'reasonably free from major defects.' These changes significantly weaken the warranty, making it subjective and unenforceable. The introduction of subjective terms makes it difficult to prove a breach of warranty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-314 implies a warranty that goods shall be merchantable, meaning they are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. The modifications significantly weaken FCC's warranty, such that the Terpene Product only needs to 'generally conform' and be in 'acceptable condition,' potentially failing to meet the implied standard of merchantability under the UCC. Delaware recognizes the UCC, as it is a virtually universally adopted law across US states.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the warranty provided by FCC. The original text states 'FCC warrants' and 'shall conform,' indicating a firm guarantee. The changed text uses weaker language such as 'FCC believes' and 'will strive to,' which introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the warranty. UCC § 2-314, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, implies a warranty that goods shall be merchantable, meaning they are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. By weakening the warranty to a subjective standard, the modified text contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability, because it makes it harder to prove that the Terpene Product does not meet the standards of being fit for ordinary use or of fair average quality.
